benefits_over_hasp: Better first-run onboarding for centrally managed teams.
Stronger hosted identity and org attribution story.
Clearer centralized governance and dashboard positioning.
MIT licensing is easier to adopt and trust than HASP's source-available direction, even though HASP now has a repo-level licensing artifact.

feature_rows.notes: Kontext resolves managed credentials from a backend/provider path; HASP stores them locally.
OIDC login, refresh token storage, and backend session identity are first-class in Kontext.
Kontext’s RFC 8693 exchange is stronger for centrally managed provider credentials.
Kontext injects credentials into the agent environment; HASP mediates secret use through brokered flows.
Direct env injection is Kontext’s default model; HASP treats workspace materialization as a warned convenience path.
Kontext’s shipped repo does not implement a comparable once/session/window approval surface.
HASP has local repo leak detection and block-by-default managed hook/deploy paths.
HASP explicitly supports audited override on repo guardrails.
Kontext is a launcher and telemetry surface, not a local managed secret lifecycle tool.
HASP has local encrypted backup/restore primitives; Kontext’s repo does not expose an equivalent.
Kontext’s repo is explicitly oriented around backend sessions and dashboard-visible traces.
Kontext’s main happy path depends on backend services; HASP can operate locally.

competitors.name: Kontext CLI
competitors.summary: Early cloud-first agent launcher with OIDC login, short-lived token exchange, direct env injection, sidecar hook telemetry, and Claude-only shipped support in the public repo.

benefits_over_hasp: Faster self-serve onboarding with a full dashboard and bundled local stack.
Stronger HTTP-native policy controls, including manual approval and rate limiting.
Bitwarden-backed external-vault fallback.
Apache-2.0 licensing is simpler for permissive-OSS buyers.

feature_rows.notes: OneCLI's primary store is the gateway/database stack, not a local encrypted vault.
OneCLI can stay local, but it does support Google OAuth for team identity.
OneCLI can refresh OAuth app tokens and do request-time external-vault lookups, but it is not centered on generic short-lived exchange.
OneCLI keeps provider keys out of HTTP clients, but it is not a general brokered local execution surface.
Neither product makes direct env injection the core happy path.
OneCLI has gateway policy rules and manual approval, but not HASP's once/session/window grant model.
HASP's repo leak scanning and block-by-default flow are absent in OneCLI.
OneCLI does not expose a repo-guardrail override model because it does not ship the repo guardrail layer.
OneCLI is a gateway and secret store, not an agent-assisted local capture system.
HASP has explicit local encrypted backup and restore primitives.
OneCLI ships a web dashboard and team-facing management surface.
OneCLI can run locally, but it still depends on a gateway/web/database stack that is heavier than HASP's local path.

competitors.name: OneCLI
competitors.summary: Self-serve AI credential gateway with one-container dashboard+gateway setup, request-time injection, policy rules, full API-call audit trail, and Bitwarden-backed human-approval integration.

benefits_over_hasp: Much broader provider catalog and provider-switching ergonomics.
Smoother ordinary developer workflow through shell auto-load and direct exec.
Short-lived credential leases for cloud and Vault-style credentials are already present.
MIT licensing, Rust implementation, and Cargo/mise distribution lower adoption friction.

feature_rows.notes: fnox can keep encrypted secrets in committed config and local providers, but it is not a dedicated local vault like HASP.
fnox inherits identity from configured providers; it does not ship its own hosted org identity plane.
fnox leases can vend temporary AWS, GCP, Azure, and Vault-style credentials.
fnox MCP exec has redaction, but the main exec/shell model materializes secrets into env vars.
Direct env injection is fnox's core compatibility path.
fnox has provider auth and MCP allowlists, not HASP-style once/session/window grants.
The current fnox scan command is present but not implemented; HASP has scan and block-by-default hook/deploy paths.
fnox does not expose a comparable audited override flow for repo leaks.
fnox supports user set/import/sync, but not HASP-style agent-assisted audited capture into managed storage.
fnox relies on committed config and provider storage; HASP has local encrypted backup/restore primitives.
Neither product ships a hosted dashboard in the evaluated surface.

feature_rows.competitor: Strong when local/encrypted providers are configured
feature_rows.notes: Both can work locally; fnox remote providers and leases depend on their upstream services.

competitors.name: fnox
competitors.summary: Flexible Rust secret manager with encrypted-in-git config, broad provider references, direct env exec, shell auto-load, MCP get/exec tools, temp-file secrets, and short-lived cloud credential leases.

benefits_over_hasp: Broader interface-agnostic coverage for HTTP(S) traffic: API, CLI, SDK, and MCP paths converge at the proxy.
First-class container sandbox mode can make egress non-cooperative by forcing TCP traffic through the proxy.
Local web UI, service catalog, proposal workflow, vault roles, request logs, and rate limits are stronger operator UX than HASP V1.
Permissive MIT Expat license for non-ee code is easier to evaluate and adopt.

feature_rows.notes: Both have local encrypted vaults.

feature_rows.competitor: Local users/session roles; Infisical ecosystem path exists but Agent Vault is local-first today
feature_rows.notes: Neither directly solves hosted org identity in the reviewed local path.

feature_rows.competitor: Scoped vault sessions for proxy access
feature_rows.notes: Both have scoped sessions, not generic provider token exchange.

feature_rows.competitor: Yes, for HTTP(S) traffic via proxy
feature_rows.notes: Agent Vault leads on HTTP; HASP leads on non-HTTP local workflows.

feature_rows.competitor: Proxy and CA env injection, not raw provider secrets
feature_rows.hasp: Convenience-only for values; wrappers set safe-mode/session env
feature_rows.notes: Agent Vault uses env for routing; HASP treats secret materialization as convenience.
Different approval grains.
HASP leads on local repo guardrails.
HASP has explicit audited override for repo guardrails.

feature_rows.competitor: Yes, via proposal/request workflow and vault credential management
feature_rows.notes: Agent Vault proposals are stronger UX; HASP capture is integrated with repo exposure and grants.

feature_rows.competitor: Not surfaced as a primary CLI feature in reviewed docs
feature_rows.notes: HASP has explicit encrypted backup/restore.

feature_rows.competitor: Local web dashboard; Infisical commercial path exists
feature_rows.notes: Agent Vault has a stronger admin UI.
Both can run locally; Agent Vault is heavier server/proxy/UI, HASP is lighter daemon/CLI.

competitors.license: MIT Expat for non-ee code; enterprise features reserved for ee/ Infisical license
competitors.name: Infisical Agent Vault
competitors.summary: Research-preview HTTP credential proxy and vault for AI agents. Agents receive scoped sessions and HTTPS proxy/CA-trust environment, while Agent Vault injects credentials at the network layer and recommends container/network egress lockdown for stronger isolation.

benefits_over_hasp: Much stronger hosted identity, attribution, and centralized AI usage reporting.
Full request/response capture, tool-use logging, and SIEM/S3 export.
Centralized MCP aggregation and access control.
Self-serve managed deployment for teams already on Tailscale.

feature_rows.notes: Aperture centralizes provider keys in the gateway; it does not ship a local encrypted vault model.
Aperture's strongest differentiator is Tailscale-backed identity and attribution.
Aperture centralizes provider API keys, but it is not presented as a generic short-lived credential exchange layer.
Aperture keeps provider keys out of clients for proxied LLM/MCP traffic, but it is not a general local execution broker.
Neither product centers its value on direct env injection.
Aperture has central grants, but not HASP's once/session/window grant model.
Repo leak detection and local block-by-default flows remain a clear HASP advantage.
Aperture's governance is centralized, but it does not expose HASP's local audited-override pattern.
Aperture is a centralized AI access plane, not an agent-assisted local secret lifecycle tool.
HASP has explicit local encrypted backup/restore primitives.
Aperture ships a centralized dashboard and analytics surface.
Aperture requires a Tailscale network and managed service; HASP can operate locally and offline.

competitors.name: Tailscale Aperture
competitors.summary: Managed beta Tailscale AI gateway for centralized LLM access, identity-backed routing, quotas, pre-LLM guardrails, configurable log retention, administrator audit logging, and experimental MCP aggregation.

features.label: Local encrypted vault as primary secret store
Short-lived credential exchange from managed providers
Brokered execution that keeps secret values out of agent-visible output
Direct env injection into agent process
Repo leak scan and block-by-default hook/deploy path
Audited override for blocked repo leaks
Agent-assisted secret capture into managed storage
Meaningful local/offline use without remote control plane

hasp.license: Source-available with FCL-1.0-ALv2 as the preferred license (ADR 0005); a repo-root LICENSE artifact now signals the current terms.
hasp.name: HASP
hasp.summary: Local-first runtime secret broker with project-scoped bindings, repo guardrails, protected agent process-tree sessions, one-time plaintext approval grants, and agent-safe brokered flows that avoid returning managed values to agent context.
